strategy: Single dense bf16 GEMM via the canonical LM head: reshape to 2D (free view) then one cuBLASLt F.linear ([out,in] weight

sources.content:
"""003_lm_head_projection_with_logit_slicing — B200 optimized kernel.

The op is a single dense bf16 GEMM: logits = hidden_states @ weightᵀ, with
hidden_states [B, S, 2048] and weight [102400, 2048]. There is no fusion, no
epilogue, and no accessible slicing lever (`run` never receives
`logits_to_keep`; reference and candidate both materialize the full [B, S, V]).

Per the B200 knowledge base (library-playbook.md): a plain dense bf16 GEMM is
won by cuBLAS/cuBLASLt — Triton reaches only 62–70% and no hand kernel reliably
beats it. So the fastest *and* numerically-faithful path is the canonical LM
head: collapse to 2D and issue one cuBLASLt GEMM via F.linear (the [out, in]
weight layout cuBLASLt is most tuned for). fp8 is deliberately avoided — it
blows the accuracy gate, and a single failed workload zeroes the whole problem.

Levers applied:
  * F.linear on a 2D view  -> the plain-GEMM cuBLAS path (no batched-matmul
    dispatch, no physical transpose; weightᵀ is a stride flip).
  * reshape() is a free view for the contiguous inputs the harness generates.
  * cuBLASLt workspace hint >= 32 MiB (below that cuBLAS silently restricts
    algorithm selection); set defensively and unit-safe.
  * bf16 in / fp32 accumulate / bf16 out — exactly the reference semantics, so
    correctness matches torch.matmul(hidden_states, weight.t()).
"""

import os

# cuBLASLt restricts its algorithm search below a 32 MiB workspace. PyTorch reads
# CUBLASLT_WORKSPACE_SIZE in KiB; 32768 KiB == 32 MiB. `setdefault` keeps any
# larger value the harness already chose, and the value is harmless under either
# unit interpretation (never large enough to OOM).
os.environ.setdefault("CUBLASLT_WORKSPACE_SIZE", "32768")

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


@torch.no_grad()
def run(hidden_states: torch.Tensor, weight: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """LM head projection: logits = hidden_states @ weightᵀ.

    Args:
        hidden_states: [batch, seq_len, hidden=2048] bf16
        weight:        [vocab=102400, hidden=2048] bf16

    Returns:
        logits: [batch, seq_len, vocab=102400] bf16
    """
    *lead, hidden = hidden_states.shape
    vocab = weight.shape[0]

    # Collapse the leading (batch, seq_len) dims to a single GEMM M dimension.
    # Contiguous input -> this is a zero-copy view; F.linear then dispatches the
    # plain [M, H] @ [H, V] cuBLASLt GEMM (weightᵀ handled as a stride flip).
    x = hidden_states.reshape(-1, hidden)
    logits = F.linear(x, weight)                 # [M, V] bf16, fp32 accumulate
    return logits.reshape(*lead, vocab)          # free view back to [B, S, V]

"""003_lm_head_projection_with_logit_slicing — B200 optimized kernel.

The op is a single dense bf16 GEMM: logits = hidden_states @ weightᵀ, with
hidden_states [B, S, 2048] and weight [102400, 2048]. There is no fusion, no
epilogue, and no accessible slicing lever (`run` never receives
`logits_to_keep`; reference and candidate both materialize the full [B, S, V]).

Per the B200 knowledge base (library-playbook.md): a plain dense bf16 GEMM is
won by cuBLAS/cuBLASLt — Triton reaches only 62–70% and no hand kernel reliably
beats it. So the fastest *and* numerically-faithful path is the canonical LM
head: collapse to 2D and issue one cuBLASLt GEMM via F.linear (the [out, in]
weight layout cuBLASLt is most tuned for). fp8 is deliberately avoided — it
blows the accuracy gate, and a single failed workload zeroes the whole problem.

Levers applied:
  * F.linear on a 2D view  -> the plain-GEMM cuBLAS path (no batched-matmul
    dispatch, no physical transpose; weightᵀ is a stride flip).
  * reshape() is a free view for the contiguous inputs the harness generates.
  * cuBLASLt workspace hint >= 32 MiB (below that cuBLAS silently restricts

  * bf16 in / fp32 accumulate / bf16 out — exactly the reference semantics, so

"""

import os

# cuBLASLt restricts its algorithm search below a 32 MiB workspace. PyTorch reads

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

    """LM head projection: logits = hidden_states @ weightᵀ.

        hidden_states: [batch, seq_len, hidden=2048] bf16

    x = hidden_states.reshape(-1, hidden)
    logits = F.linear(x, weight)                 # [M, V] bf16, fp32 accumulate
    return logits.reshape(*lead, vocab)          # free view back to [B, S, V]
